key_reference_points: AHRS failure causes loss of attitude indicator
Red X on PFD attitude display indicates AHRS failure
AHRS provides attitude (pitch and roll) and heading information
AHRS stands for Attitude and Heading Reference System
Without AHRS, pilot loses primary attitude reference on glass cockpit displays

questions.question: How will loss of a magnetometer affect the AHRS operation? (FAA- H-8083-6) Heading information will be lost.
key_reference_points: AHRS (Attitude and Heading Reference System) uses magnetometer for heading information
Magnetometer failure results in loss of magnetic heading data
AHRS will still provide attitude (pitch and roll) information without magnetometer
GPS track may be used as backup heading reference in some systems
Magnetometer provides magnetic north reference for directional guidance
questions.exam_tip: Be prepared to answer questions about any and all equipment installed in the aircraft. For example, if your aircraft has an autopilot, have in- depth knowledge of its operation, even if you rarely use it.

questions.question: Does the nose wheel turn when the rudder is depressed in flight? (AFM)
key_reference_points: Nose wheel steering is typically connected to rudder pedals on the ground
In flight, rudder pedals control the rudder for yaw
Nose wheel steering mechanism is usually designed to be active only when weight is on the wheels
Aircraft-specific systems vary - always consult the AFM/POH for your aircraft
Some aircraft use bungee or spring systems that center the nose wheel in flight

questions.question: If the braking system is not functioning, will the parking brake work? (AFM)
key_reference_points: Parking brake uses the same hydraulic system as main brakes
If hydraulic brake system fails, parking brake will also be inoperative
Parking brake works by mechanically holding brake pressure applied by the pilot
No independent backup system exists for parking brake in most light aircraft
Always consult aircraft-specific AFM/POH for brake system details

questions.question: If the brakes on the left side (pilot) are not functioning, will the brakes on the right side also be inoperative? (AFM)
key_reference_points: Most single-engine aircraft have dual independent brake systems (left and right)
Each pilot's toe brakes typically control their respective side's wheel brake
If left brakes fail, right brakes should still function independently
Brake systems are hydraulically separate - one side's failure doesn't affect the other
Know your specific aircraft's brake system configuration per the AFM

questions.question: Explain the procedure for starting your airplane with external power. (AFM)
key_reference_points: Verify external power unit voltage matches aircraft system (typically 24V or 12V)
Master switch OFF before connecting external power
Connect positive lead first, then negative/ground
Follow AFM checklist for external power start procedure
Disconnect external power before turning master switch ON or after engine start
Ensure proper polarity to prevent electrical system damage

questions.question: You cannot start your airplane due to a low battery, so you request an external start via a ground power cart. What problems might still occur after the engine has started? (AFM)
key_reference_points: Aircraft battery may not hold charge - could die again if engine stops
Alternator may not immediately charge a deeply discharged battery
Battery may be damaged and unable to accept a charge
Electrical system may be unreliable for the remainder of the flight
Monitor ammeter/voltmeter closely after start to verify charging
Consider having battery tested/replaced before further flight

questions.question: In the event of an electrical system failure, what time duration can you reasonably expect electrical power from the battery? (AFM)
key_reference_points: Typical light aircraft battery provides 30 minutes or less of electrical power after alternator failure
Duration depends on electrical load - reduce load by turning off non-essential equipment
Check your specific aircraft's AFM/POH for exact battery endurance times
Ammeter will show discharge (negative) when running on battery alone
Priority loads: radios for communication, transponder, essential flight instruments

questions.question: What effect would positioning the master switch to the “Off” position have on aircraft systems while in flight? (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Engine continues to run - ignition system is powered by magnetos, independent of electrical system
All electrical equipment becomes inoperative (radios, lights, electric flaps, fuel pumps)
Electrically-driven gyro instruments (attitude indicator, heading indicator) will spin down
Battery stops charging and alternator output is disconnected
Pitot heat, if electric, will stop functioning

questions.question: What instruments are affected when the pitot tube freezes? Static port freezes? (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Pitot tube blockage affects ONLY the airspeed indicator
Static port blockage affects airspeed indicator, altimeter, and vertical speed indicator (VSI)
With static port blocked: altimeter freezes, VSI shows zero, airspeed indicator gives erroneous readings
Alternate static source (if available) typically reads slightly higher altitude due to lower cabin pressure
Pitot heat can prevent or clear pitot tube ice blockage
Use alternate static source or break VSI glass as emergency static source

questions.question: What is the purpose of the alternate static source? (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Alternate static source provides backup if primary static port becomes blocked (ice, debris)
Located inside cockpit where pressure is slightly lower than outside due to venturi effect
When activated: altimeter reads slightly HIGH, airspeed reads slightly HIGH, VSI shows momentary climb
Must be used if static port blockage is suspected to restore flight instrument function
Some aircraft use breaking the VSI glass as an alternate static source method

questions.question: If you set the altimeter from 29.15 to 29.85, what change occurs? (FAA-H-8083-25)
key_reference_points: Increasing altimeter setting from 29.15 to 29.85 equals +0.70 inches Hg change
Each 0.01 inch Hg change equals approximately 10 feet of indicated altitude
0.70 inch change × 10 feet = 700 feet INCREASE in indicated altitude
Higher pressure setting = higher altitude indication (airplane appears higher)
Memory aid: 'High to low, look out below' - going from high to low pressure without correcting causes indicated altitude to read higher than actual

questions.question: During the “before takeoff” magneto check, you notice that the right magneto is extremely rough. Explain what the problem could be and what actions you will take next. (AFM)
key_reference_points: Rough mag can indicate fouled spark plugs, broken plug wire, or impulse coupling issue
Do NOT takeoff with a rough-running magneto - this is a no-go item
Try clearing fouled plugs: lean mixture aggressively, run engine at higher RPM for 30-60 seconds
Recheck magnetos after attempting to clear - RPM drop should be within limits (typically 50-175 RPM)
If roughness persists after clearing attempt, taxi back and have maintenance inspect
Maximum allowable RPM difference between magnetos is typically 50 RPM (check specific AFM)

questions.question: Automation in the cockpit has made aviation safer. Does total risk increase or decrease when “passively” monitoring an automated system for faults or abnormalities? How can you mitigate that risk? (FAA-H- 8083-2)
key_reference_points: Total risk INCREASES when passively monitoring automated systems due to complacency and reduced situational awareness
Automation complacency occurs when pilots over-trust systems and fail to detect malfunctions or errors
Mitigation: Stay actively engaged by cross-checking automation with raw data and backup instruments
Mitigation: Maintain proficiency in manual flying skills through regular practice without automation
Mitigation: Use a structured scan pattern to verify automation is performing as expected
Always be prepared to take immediate manual control if automation fails or behaves unexpectedly
